FelixArg's blog

Codeforces Round 1079 (Div. 1, Div. 2) Editorial
By FelixArg, 6 weeks ago, translation,

Thank you for participating! We hope you enjoyed the problems.

2197A - Friendly Numbers

Tutorial

2197A - Friendly Numbers

To solve this problem, it is sufficient to explicitly check that the number ii meets the condition for all ii from xx to x+83x+83. To verify this, we need to explicitly calculate i−d(i)i−d(i). Now let's prove this.

Notice that numbers greater than 2⋅1092⋅109 definitely do not suit us, since the sum of the digits of the number 2⋅109+y⋅1092⋅109+y⋅109 does not exceed 9⋅9+2+y=83+y9⋅9+2+y=83+y, while the value of ii in this case will be at least (2+y)⋅109(2+y)⋅109, which obviously cannot be suitable.

Thus, the maximum sum of the numbers we are interested in does not exceed 8383, which means that checking is relevant between xx and x+83x+83

Solution
t = int(input())
for tt in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    ans = 0
 
    def dig_sum(n):
        s = str(n)
        sum = 0
        for j in s:
            sum += ord(j) - ord('0')
        return sum
 
    for i in range(n, n + 200):
        if i - dig_sum(i) == n:
            ans += 1
    print(ans)

2197B - Array and Permutation

Tutorial

2197B - Array and Permutation

Let's take a closer look at what the array looks like after such operations. I claim that it will have the form q1,q2,…,qnq1,q2,…,qn, where the element at position qiqi in permutation pp is not to the right of the element qi+1qi+1. The intuition and proof are as follows:

Essentially, at each iteration, each element of the permutation is located on some segment of the array aa, in the corresponding initial order. Then, after each operation, one segment simply increases in length by 11, while the other decreases by 11 (or is removed). Therefore, these segments cannot change their order. This means that the array after any number of operations will have the form stated above. It is also clear how to obtain any such array using the operations described in the problem statement

Solution
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
#define all(a) a.begin(), a.end()
#define rall(a) a.rbegin(), a.rend()
#define len(a) int(a.size())
using ll = long long;
using ull = unsigned long long;
using ld = long double;
 
using namespace std;
 
const int mod = 998244353;
 
const int N = 1e6 + 228;
 
pair<int, int> zero = {-1, N};
 
 
void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    map<int, int> pos;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        int a;
        cin >> a;
        pos[a] = i;
    }
    int lst = -1;
    vector<int> b(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) cin >> b[i];
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        if (lst > pos[b[i]]) {
            cout << "NO\n";
            return;
        }
        lst = pos[b[i]];
    }
    cout << "YES\n";
}
 
int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
 
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

2196A - Game with a Fraction

Tutorial

2196A - Game with a Fraction

The solution is very simple, it is necessary to check that:

  • p<qp<q
  • min(p2,q3)≥q−pmin(p2,q3)≥q−p

If both conditions are met, then Bob wins, otherwise, Alice wins.

The first condition is quite obvious, if it is not met, then Alice will always decrease qq if possible, leading to p≥qp≥q, so the fraction will always be either >23>23 or =0=0 (after all moves).

The second condition states that no matter what move Alice makes, there is always a move for Bob that maintains the original difference q−pq−p. There will be a moment during the game when p=2⋅min(p2,q3)p=2⋅min(p2,q3), because 2⋅min(p2,q3)≤p2⋅min(p2,q3)≤p, and at that moment, with such a difference, the fraction will exactly equal 2323.

The solution works in O(1)O(1)

Solution
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
#define int long long
 
void solve(){
	int p, q;
	cin >> p >> q;
	if (p < q && min(p / 2, q / 3) >= q - p){
		cout << "Bob\n";
	}
	else{
		cout << "Alice\n";
	}
}
 
signed main()
{
#ifdef FELIX
	auto _clock_start = chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
#endif
	ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(0);
	cout.tie(0);
 
	int t = 1;
	cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		solve();
	}
 
#ifdef FELIX
	cerr << "Executed in " << chrono::duration_cast<chrono::milliseconds>(
		chrono::high_resolution_clock::now()
			- _clock_start).count() << "ms." << endl;
#endif
	return 0;
}

2196B - Another Problem about Beautiful Pairs

Tutorial

2196B - Another Problem about Beautiful Pairs

Consider the following solution:

For each ii, we will check all possible candidates jj that can form beautiful pairs. To do this, we will iterate through such j=i+k⋅aij=i+k⋅ai, where k>0k>0.

This solution works slowly for small aiai. Let's try to speed it up using square root decomposition. We will introduce a parameter BB and will iterate through candidates for ii in different ways depending on whether ai<Bai<B or ai≥Bai≥B:

  • If ai≥Bai≥B, we will iterate through candidates in both directions from ii (not just forward), meaning for each such ii, the set of candidates will be i+k⋅aii+k⋅ai, where kk is any integer;
  • If ai<Bai<B, we will also iterate through candidates only forward, but we will introduce an additional restriction on kk, namely k<Bk<B.

Let's estimate the maximum possible number of candidates for these two cases:

  • In the first case, for all such ii where ai≥Bai≥B, it is nBnB, since we move in steps of BB;
  • In the second case, it is clear that there are no more than BB candidates, as we have introduced such a restriction ourselves.

If we choose B=⌈n−−√⌉B=⌈n⌉, then the number of candidates for each case is no more than ⌈n−−√⌉⌈n⌉, so the entire algorithm solves the problem in O(nn−−√)O(nn).

Let's think about why such an algorithm will be correct. Consider the cases of a beautiful pair (i,j)(i,j), depending on the values of aiai and ajaj:

  • If ai<Bai<B and aj<Baj<B, then the pair will be counted when we iterate through candidates for ii;
  • If ai≥Bai≥B and aj<Baj<B, then the pair will be counted when we iterate through candidates for jj;
  • If ai<Bai<B and aj≥Baj≥B, then the pair will be counted when we iterate through candidates for jj;

Note that there is no case where ai≥Bai≥B and aj≥Baj≥B, since we chose B=⌈n−−√⌉B=⌈n⌉, which means that in such a pair ai⋅aj≥nai⋅aj≥n, and the maximum value of j−i=n−1j−i=n−1.

Therefore, our algorithm is correct

Solution
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
#define int long long
 
const int SQ = 500;
 
void solve(){
  int n;
  cin >> n;
  vector<int> a(n);
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
    cin >> a[i];
  }
 
  int ans = 0;
 
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
    if (a[i] >= SQ){
      for (int j = 1; i + a[i] * j < n; j++){
        if (a[i + a[i] * j] == j){
          ans++;
        }
      }
      for (int j = 1; i - a[i] * j >= 0; j++){
        if (a[i - a[i] * j] == j){
          ans++;
        }
      }
    }
    else{
      for (int j = 1; i + a[i] * j < n && j < SQ; j++){
        if (a[i + a[i] * j] == j){
          ans++;
        }
      }
    }
  }
 
  cout << ans << '\n';
}
 
signed main()
{
#ifdef FELIX
	auto _clock_start = chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
#endif
	ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(0);
	cout.tie(0);
 
	int t = 1;
	cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		solve();
	}
 
#ifdef FELIX
	cerr << "Executed in " << chrono::duration_cast<chrono::milliseconds>(
		chrono::high_resolution_clock::now()
			- _clock_start).count() << "ms." << endl;
#endif
	return 0;
}

2196C1 - Interactive Graph (Simple Version)

Tutorial

2196C1 - Interactive Graph (Simple Version)

Let's solve the problem using binary search. For each edge, there exists a path that consists solely of that edge, so let's simply find that path and remember that this edge is in our graph. Initially, we set the boundaries l=0l=0 and r=230r=230 and will search for the first path consisting of 11 edge that starts at vertex 11. Once we find it, we will add the corresponding edge to the answer. After that, we continue searching for the next path of one edge that starts at vertex 11 with a new binary search, taking into account that all paths starting from the first found edge are no longer of interest to us. When two edges are known, we will ignore all paths that start on them, and so on. Ultimately, we will find all edges from vertex 11 (the last binary search will not find an edge from vertex 11). After this, we will continue searching for all edges from vertex 22, 33, and so on.

As a result, we will need logA⋅(n+m)log⁡A⋅(n+m) queries. Since A=230A=230, this fits well within the query limits of the problem

Solution
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
#define int long long
 
const int INF = 1'000'000'007;
 
vector<int> ask(int k){
	cout << "? " << k << endl;
	int l;
	cin >> l;
	if (l == -1){
		exit(0);
	}
	vector<int> res(l);
	for (int i = 0; i < l; i++){
		cin >> res[i];
	}
	return res;
}
 
void solve(){
	int n;
	cin >> n;
 
	vector<pair<int, int>> edges;
 
	for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
		int prev = -1;
		while(1){
			int l = 1, r = (1 << 30);
			while(l <= r){
				int m = l + (r - l) / 2;
				auto path = ask(m);
				if (!path.empty() && path[0] < i + 1){
					l = m + 1;
					continue;
				}
				if (path.empty() || path[0] > i + 1 || (path.size() > 1 && path[1] > prev)){
					r = m - 1;
				}
				else{
					l = m + 1;
				}
			}
 
			auto path = ask(l);
			if (path.size() > 1){
				edges.emplace_back(path[0], path[1]);
				prev = path[1];
			}
			else{
				break;
			}
		}
	}
 
	cout << "! " << edges.size() << endl;
	for (auto [u, v] : edges){
		cout << u << ' ' << v << endl;
	}
}
 
signed main()
{
#ifdef FELIX
	auto _clock_start = chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
#endif
	ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(0);
	cout.tie(0);
 
	int t = 1;
	cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		solve();
	}
 
#ifdef FELIX
	cerr << "Executed in " << chrono::duration_cast<chrono::milliseconds>(
		chrono::high_resolution_clock::now()
			- _clock_start).count() << "ms." << endl;
#endif
	return 0;
}

2196C2 - Interactive Graph (Hard Version)

Tutorial

2196C2 - Interactive Graph (Hard Version)

Let's start asking for paths in order starting from 22, since path 11 consists of a single vertex 11. If we ask for paths one by one (that is, each subsequent one), then during our questioning, the path will change in one of two ways:

  • a vertex has been added to the previous path;
  • a suffix of vertices has been removed from the previous path and a new one has been added.

Note that when the second point occurs, for all the vertices that are removed from the suffix, we can determine the number of paths that start from this vertex and end at some other vertex, let's denote this as CvCv. Therefore, if at some point we visit a vertex for the second time, we can immediately skip these CvCv paths and move on to the next one in lexicographic order. Thus, for each query, we either learn about one edge or move to another connected component, so we need exactly n+mn+m questions

Solution
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
vector<int> ask(int k){
	cout << "? " << k << endl;
	int cnt;
	cin >> cnt;
	vector<int> res(cnt);
	for (int i = 0; i < cnt; i++){
		cin >> res[i];
		res[i]--;
	}
	return res;
}
 
void solve(){
	int n;
	cin >> n;
 
	int m = 0;
	vector<vector<int>> g(n);
	vector<int> cnt(n, -1);
 
	function<void(int)> calc = [&](int v){
		if (cnt[v] != -1){
			return;
		}
		cnt[v] = 1;
		for (int to : g[v]){
			calc(to);
			cnt[v] += cnt[to];
		}
	};
 
	vector<int> prev = {0};
	int k = 2;
 
	while(1){
		auto cur = ask(k);
		if (cur.size() == 0){
			break;
		}
		int com_pref = 0;
		for (int i = 0; i < min(prev.size(), cur.size()); i++){
			if (prev[i] != cur[i]){
				break;
			}
			com_pref++;
		}
		if (prev.size() > com_pref){
			calc(prev[com_pref]);
		}
 
		if (com_pref > 0){
			g[cur[com_pref - 1]].push_back(cur[com_pref]);
			m++;
		}
		if (cnt[cur[com_pref]] == -1){
			k++;
		}
		else{
			k += cnt[cur[com_pref]];
		}
		prev = cur;
	}
 
	cout << "! " << m << endl;
	for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
		for (int to : g[i]){
			cout << i + 1 << ' ' << to + 1 << endl;
		}
	}
}
 
signed main()
{
#ifdef FELIX
	auto _clock_start = chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
#endif
	ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(0);
	cout.tie(0);
 
	int tests = 1;
	cin >> tests;
	for (int test = 0; test < tests; test++){
		solve();
	}
#ifdef FELIX
	cerr << "Executed in " << chrono::duration_cast<chrono::milliseconds>(
		chrono::high_resolution_clock::now()
			- _clock_start).count() << "ms." << endl;
#endif
	return 0;
}

2196D - Double Bracket Sequence

Tutorial

2196D - Double Bracket Sequence

The problem has a fairly simple solution with a time complexity of O(|s|3)O(|s|3) using dynamic programming of the form dp[i][bal1][bal2]dp[i][bal1][bal2]—the minimum number of changes required after processing the first ii characters, where the balance of the subsequence of round brackets is bal1bal1, and the balance of the subsequence of square brackets is bal2bal2. The transitions are quite trivial (from each state, we try to process the next bracket by either changing it or not changing it).

However, this solution does not help when the constraints are |s|<=106|s|<=106 (though it is useful for stress testing).

Another solution suggests using a greedy approach. Ultimately, each bracket must have a pair, and for each pair, either 00, 11, or 22 actions are needed to make the pair correct. For example, for the pair "()", 00 actions are needed; for the pair "[)", 11 action is needed; and for the pair "][", 22 actions are needed. Let's assume we have optimally paired the brackets (such that this pairing achieves the minimum number of actions); then an important observation is that there can be at most one pair that requires 22 actions, because otherwise, we could form pairs differently, which would not worsen the answer.

We would also like to maximize the number of pairs that require 00 actions. The simplest way to do this is to find a subsequence of round brackets that forms the longest valid parentheses subsequence (LPS), and do the same for square brackets. The remaining brackets that do not belong to any LPS will form pairs that require either 11 or 22 actions. Here, we need to check whether there will be that one pair that requires 22 actions or not. It turns out that it is sufficient to check that the remaining brackets form a sequence where closing brackets come first, followed by opening brackets, with an odd number of closing and opening brackets, for example:

"))]))[[("

Here, the opening brackets (55 in total) come first, followed by the closing brackets (33 in total), and for such a sequence, there will be a pair that requires 22 actions.

In any other case, there will be no pairs requiring 22 actions.

This solution works in O(|s|)O(|s|)

Solution
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
#define int long long
 
const int INF = 1'000'000'007;
 
void solve(){
	int n;
	cin >> n;
	string s;
	cin >> s;
 
	vector<int> stay(n);
 
	auto delete_cbs = [&](char open, char close){
		vector<int> st;
		for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
			if (s[i] == close){
				if (!st.empty() && s[st.back()] == open){
					st.pop_back();
				}
				else{
					st.push_back(i);
				}
			}
			else if (s[i] == open){
				st.push_back(i);
			}
		}
		for (int x : st){
			stay[x] = 1;
		}
	};
 
	delete_cbs('(', ')');
	delete_cbs('[', ']');
 
	int last_open = 0;
	int cnt_open = 0, cnt_close = 0;
	int ans = 1;
 
	for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
		if (!stay[i]){
			continue;
		}
		if (s[i] == '(' || s[i] == '['){
			cnt_open++;
			last_open = 1;
		}
		else{
			cnt_close++;
			if (last_open){
				ans = 0;
			}
			last_open = 0;
		}
	}
	if (cnt_open % 2 == 0 || cnt_close % 2 == 0){
		ans = 0;
	}
	ans += (cnt_open + cnt_close) / 2;
	cout << ans << '\n';
}
 
signed main()
{
#ifdef FELIX
	auto _clock_start = chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
#endif
	ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(0);
	cout.tie(0);
 
	int t = 1;
	cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		solve();
	}
 
#ifdef FELIX
	cerr << "Executed in " << chrono::duration_cast<chrono::milliseconds>(
		chrono::high_resolution_clock::now()
			- _clock_start).count() << "ms." << endl;
#endif
	return 0;
}

2196E1 - Fuzzy Concatenation (Easy Version)

Tutorial

2196E1 - Fuzzy Concatenation (Easy Version)

The following greedy algorithm works: we will find the longest prefix of the string tt such that this prefix is contained in ss as a substring (possibly with one character replaced), and then remove this prefix from tt and repeat until tt is empty. The number of such "pieces" will be the answer. This greediness is correct because if we take a prefix of shorter length, the next piece can only decrease in size, not increase.

Now we need to learn how to quickly solve the following problem:

For a fixed number ll, find the maximum rr such that there exists a number ii and a string cc, for which the string tltl+1…ti−1cti+1…trtltl+1…ti−1cti+1…tr is a substring of the string ss.

Solving this problem involves using root optimization. Let BB be the constant we chose for our root. Let dd denote r−l+1r−l+1 for optimal i,ci,c (in other words, how many characters we processed in one iteration of the greedy algorithm). We want to handle the case d<Bd<B in O(d)O(d) time.

Let's store the hash of each substring of the string ss of length at most BB, where we have removed one of the elements. There are a total of O(n⋅B2)O(n⋅B2) such segments. Thus, for fixed i,di,d, where d<Bd<B, we can check in O(1)O(1) time whether a given string is a substring of ss by verifying that the hash of the string without the character cc is in the set of precomputed hashes. This allows us to solve the case when d<Bd<B in O(d2)O(d2) time. We can iterate ii from ll to the maximum accumulated length and increase the optimal dd by exactly 11 if we find this substring for the given ii. Smaller rr values are not of interest to us. Overall, this part of the solution works in O(m⋅B)O(m⋅B) time, as we increase rr no more than mm times in total, and each check works in dd time, since there are at most BB interesting values of ii in each iteration.

If d≥Bd≥B, we can explicitly iterate over the starting point of the substring in the string ss. Let's denote this starting point of the substring as jj. Let d1d1 be lcp(sj…sn,tl…tm)lcp⁡(sj…sn,tl…tm). Then for a fixed starting point jj, we can add at most d1+1+lcp(sj+d1+1…sn,tl+d1+1…tm)d1+1+lcp⁡(sj+d1+1…sn,tl+d1+1…tm). The lcplcp can be easily computed in O(1)O(1) time using a suffix array for the string s\#ts\#t. Thus, this part of the solution works in at most O(n⋅mB)O(n⋅mB) time, as this search algorithm will be run no more than mBmB times, and the iteration itself works in O(n)O(n) time.

The asymptotic complexity of the solution is O(n⋅B2+n⋅mB+m⋅B)O(n⋅B2+n⋅mB+m⋅B). With B=50B=50, this solution should fit well within the problem's constraints.

There is also a solution that runs in 26⋅m⋅log2(n+m)+log(n)⋅(n+m)26⋅m⋅log2⁡(n+m)+log⁡(n)⋅(n+m), which is based on a complete solution but uses hashes

2196E2 - Fuzzy Concatenation (Hard version)

Tutorial

2196E2 - Fuzzy Concatenation (Hard version)

The following greedy algorithm works: we will find the longest prefix of the string tt such that this prefix is contained in ss as a substring (possibly with one character replaced) and then remove this prefix from tt and repeat until tt is empty. The number of such "pieces" will be the answer. This greediness is correct because if we take a prefix of shorter length, the next piece can only decrease in size, not increase.

To efficiently find the longest prefix, we will use the following algorithm:

We will start from the beginning of the prefix and try to change the current character to some character from the alphabet, and then attempt to find the LCP of the string tt with the modified character and some suffix of the string ss. Let f(t′,pos)f(t′,pos) denote the time to find such an LCP if a character in the string is changed at position pospos to some character from the alphabet; then the time complexity of the entire algorithm can be estimated as O(|t|⋅∑|t|i=1f(t′,i))O(|t|⋅∑i=1|t|f(t′,i)).

We will use a suffix array. We will construct it for the string s+"#"+ts+"#"+t, and also compute the LCP for this array. Additionally, we will compute the reverse array sa_revsa_rev: that is, such that sa_rev[i]sa_rev[i] contains the position of the suffix of length ii in the suffix array sasa.

Let tsufftsuff be the suffix of the original tt, where we need to search for the maximum "piece". Also, let tsuff,pstsuff,ps be the suffix of the original tt, in which one character was changed at position psps.

In the suffix array, there are two classes of suffixes: for ss and for tt. We will separate the positions of all suffixes for ss into a separate array. Now let's search for our maximum "piece" in tsuff,pstsuff,ps. We will perform a binary search on the suffix array to determine where the position of the string tsuff,pstsuff,ps would be in it if we wanted to insert tsuff,pstsuff,ps into the suffix array constructed solely from ss.

Next, we need to understand how the check for this binary search will look. Suppose we want to check during the binary search whether the position after saisai is the position we need; let sajsaj and saksak be the positions of the nearest suffixes to the left and right that belong to ss. We need to check that saj≤tsuff,ps≤saksaj≤tsuff,ps≤sak. It seems that comparing such strings cannot be done faster than loglog, but since we do not have arbitrary strings, we can do the following:

We will find the LCP of sajsaj and tsufftsuff (we can do this because sasa is constructed from all suffixes of ss and tt, and we can find the LCP of any two arbitrary suffixes in the suffix array in O(1)O(1)). If this LCP is greater than psps for the current tsuff,pstsuff,ps, then we know the relationship between the strings sajsaj and tsuff,pstsuff,ps (greater, less, or equal). If the LCP is less, we will check that the corresponding character of sajsaj at position psps is equal to the corresponding character in tsuff,pstsuff,ps (if it is not equal, we again know the relationship between the strings).

If it turns out to be equal, we need to compare the strings sasa_revsaj+pssasa_revsaj+ps and tsuff+pstsuff+ps. This can also be done in O(1)O(1) by looking at their positions in the suffix array sasa.

After we have found the required position in the suffix using binary search, we can similarly calculate the maximum LCP of tsuff,pstsuff,ps and the substring of ss also in O(1)O(1).

Thus, f(t′,pos)f(t′,pos) can be estimated as O(log(|s|+|t|)⋅k)O(log⁡(|s|+|t|)⋅k), where kk is the size of the alphabet.

Therefore, the problem can be solved in O(|t|⋅log(|s|+|t|)⋅k)O(|t|⋅log⁡(|s|+|t|)⋅k)

Solution
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
template <typename T>
struct SparseTable{
	int n, r;
	vector<vector<T>> t;
	void build(vector<T> &a) {
		n = a.size();
		r = 32 - __builtin_clz(n);
		t.resize(r, vector<T>(n + 1));
		for(int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) t[0][i] = a[i - 1];
		for(int k = 1; k < r; ++k) {
			for(int i = 1; i + (1 << k) - 1 <= n; ++i) {
				t[k][i] = f(t[k - 1][i], t[k - 1][i + (1 << (k - 1))]);
			}
		}
	}
 
	T query(int l, int r) {
		int k = 31 - __builtin_clz(r - l + 1);
		return f(t[k][l], t[k][r - (1 << k) + 1]);
	}
 
	inline T f(T x, T y){
		return min(x, y);
	}
};
 
struct SuffixArray{
    int n;
    vector<int> sa;
    vector<int> lcp;
 
    vector<int> _s;
    vector<int> _inv_sa;
    SparseTable<int> _st_lcp;
 
    SuffixArray(){}
 
    void build(const string& s){
        _s.assign(s.size(), 0);
        for (int i = 0; i < s.size(); i++){
            _s[i] = s[i];
        }
        _build(_s);
        _build_lcp_array();
        _st_lcp.build(lcp);
    }
 
    void build(const vector<int>& s){
        _s.assign(s.size(), 0);
        for (int i = 0; i < s.size(); i++){
            _s[i] = s[i];
        }
        _build(_s);
        _build_lcp_array();
        _st_lcp.build(lcp);
    }
 
    void _build(const vector<int>& s){
        map<int, int> mp;
        for (int ch : s){
            mp[ch] = 0;
        }
        int cc = 1;
        for (auto& [key, val]: mp){
            val = cc++;
        }
 
        n = s.size() + 1;
        vector<int> c(n);
        sa.assign(n, 0);
 
        for (int i = 0; i < s.size(); i++){
            c[i] = mp[s[i]];
            sa[i] = i;
        }
        c[n - 1] = 0;
        sa[n - 1] = n - 1;
 
        for (int i = 1; (i >> 1) < n; i <<= 1){
            if (i != 1){
                for (int j = 0; j < n; j++){
                    sa[j] = (sa[j] - (i >> 1) + n) % n;
                }
            }
            vector<int> cnt(n);
            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++){
                cnt[c[sa[j]]]++;
            }
            int su = 0;
            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++){
                su += cnt[j];
                cnt[j] = su - cnt[j];
            }
            vector<int> new_sa(n);
            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++){
                new_sa[cnt[c[sa[j]]]++] = sa[j];
            }
            sa.swap(new_sa);
 
            if (i != 1){
                vector<int> new_c(n);
                new_c[sa[0]] = 0;
                for (int j = 1; j < n; j++){
                    if (c[sa[j]] > c[sa[j - 1]] || c[(sa[j] + (i >> 1)) % n] > c[(sa[j - 1] + (i >> 1)) % n]){
                        new_c[sa[j]] = new_c[sa[j - 1]] + 1;
                    }
                    else{
                        new_c[sa[j]] = new_c[sa[j - 1]];
                    }
                }
                c.swap(new_c);
            }
        }
    }
 
    void _build_lcp_array(){
        lcp.assign(n - 1, 0);
        _inv_sa.assign(n, 0);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            _inv_sa[sa[i]] = i;
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            if (_inv_sa[i] == n - 1){
                continue;
            }
            lcp[_inv_sa[i]] = 0;
            if (i > 0 && _inv_sa[i - 1] != n - 1){
                lcp[_inv_sa[i]] = max(0, lcp[_inv_sa[i - 1]] - 1);
            }
 
            int len = min(n - 1 - i, n - 1 - sa[_inv_sa[i] + 1]);
            while (lcp[_inv_sa[i]] < len && _s[i + lcp[_inv_sa[i]]] == _s[sa[_inv_sa[i] + 1] + lcp[_inv_sa[i]]]){
                lcp[_inv_sa[i]]++;
            }
        }
    }
 
    int get_lcp(int i, int j){
        assert(i != j);
        i = _inv_sa[i];
        j = _inv_sa[j];
        if (i > j){
            swap(i, j);
        }
        j--;
        return _st_lcp.query(i + 1, j + 1);
    }
};
 
void solve(){
    int n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;
    string s, t;
    cin >> s >> t;
 
    string all = s + "%" + t;
    SuffixArray suff_array;
    suff_array.build(all);
    all += '#';
 
    vector<int> from_s;
    for (int i = 0; i < n + m + 2; i++){
        if (suff_array.sa[i] < n){
            from_s.push_back(i);
        }
    }
 
    function<pair<int, int>(int, int, char, int)> compare_suff = [&](int start, int pos, char ch, int suff_num){
        if (suff_num == 0 || suff_num > n){
            return pair<int, int>{1, 0};
        }
        pos -= start;
        suff_num--;
        suff_num = suff_array.sa[from_s[suff_num]];
        
        int lcp1 = suff_array.get_lcp(suff_num, n + 1 + start);
        if (lcp1 < pos){
            char ch1 = all[suff_num + lcp1];
            char ch2 = all[n + 1 + start + lcp1];
            if (ch1 < ch2){
                return pair<int, int>{1, lcp1};
            }
            else{
                return pair<int, int>{-1, lcp1};
            }
        }
        else if (lcp1 > pos){
            char ch1 = all[suff_num + pos];
            char ch2;
            int len = pos;
            if (ch1 != ch){
                ch2 = ch;
            }
            else{
                len = lcp1;
                ch1 = all[suff_num + lcp1];
                ch2 = all[n + 1 + start + lcp1];
            }
            if (ch1 < ch2){
                return pair<int, int>{1, len};
            }
            else{
                return pair<int, int>{-1, len};
            }
        }
 
        char ch1 = all[suff_num + lcp1];
        char ch2 = ch;
        if (ch1 < ch2){
            return pair<int, int>{1, pos};
        }
        else if (ch1 > ch2){
            return pair<int, int>{-1, pos};
        }
 
        int lcp2 = suff_array.get_lcp(suff_num + lcp1 + 1, n + 1 + start + lcp1 + 1);
 
        ch1 = all[suff_num + lcp1 + 1 + lcp2];
        ch2 = all[n + 1 + start + lcp1 + 1 + lcp2];
 
        if (ch1 < ch2){
            return pair<int, int>{1, pos + 1 + lcp2};
        }
        else{
            return pair<int, int>{-1, pos + 1 + lcp2};
        }
    };
 
    function<int(int, int, char)> get_max_len = [&](int start, int pos, char ch){
        int suff_len = n + 1 + start;
 
        int l = 0, r = from_s.size();
        while(l <= r){
            int m = l + (r - l) / 2;
            if (compare_suff(start, pos, ch, m).first == -1){
                r = m - 1;
            }
            else{
                l = m + 1;
            }
        }
        int res = 0;
        res = max(res, compare_suff(start, pos, ch, l - 1).second);
        res = max(res, compare_suff(start, pos, ch, l).second);
        return res;
    };
 
    int ans = 0;
    int start = 0;
    int pred = -1;
 
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++){
        int mx_len = 0;
        for (char ch = 'a'; ch <= 'z'; ch++){
            mx_len = max(mx_len, get_max_len(start, i, ch));
        }
        pred = max(pred, mx_len);
        if (mx_len < i - start + 1){
            ans++;
            start += pred;
            pred = -1;
            i = start - 1;
            continue;
        }
    }
    if (start != m){
        ans++;
    }
 
    cout << ans << '\n';
}
 
signed main()
{
#ifdef FELIX
	auto _clock_start = chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
#endif
	ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(0);
	cout.tie(0);
 
	int t = 1;
	cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		solve();
	}
 
#ifdef FELIX
	cerr << "Executed in " << chrono::duration_cast<chrono::milliseconds>(
		chrono::high_resolution_clock::now()
			- _clock_start).count() << "ms." << endl;
#endif
	return 0;
}

2196F - Indivisible

Tutorial

2196F - Indivisible

First solution:

Let's start by solving the problem for odd nn. Intuitively, if for an odd nn we can make all vertex degrees almost equal and as large as possible, there should be a good chance that the set of degrees will be indivisible.

We divide the sum of all degrees, 2m2m, by nn with a remainder. Let 2m=q⋅n+r2m=q⋅n+r. 0≤r<n0≤r<n.

We can make the vertex degrees almost equal if they are q,…,q,q+1,…,q+1q,…,q,q+1,…,q+1, where q+1q+1 appears rr times, and qq appears n−rn−r times. A graph with such degrees can always be constructed; let's consider two cases:

  1. qq and rr are even numbers. Mentally arrange the vertices in a circle from 11 to nn, where nn and 11 will also be neighboring vertices. Connect vertices ii and jj with an edge if the minimum distance between them around the circle does not exceed q2q2. After this, each vertex will have a degree equal to qq. Then we add r2r2 edges between pairs of vertices 1<=>n2+1,…,r<=>n2+r1<=>n2+1,…,r<=>n2+r.
  2. qq and rr are odd numbers. Since nn is odd and q<nq<n, it is also true that q+1<nq+1<n, which means we can construct a configuration from the first case where each vertex degree is q+1q+1. After that, we remove edges 1<=>2,…,n−r−1<=>n−r1<=>2,…,n−r−1<=>n−r.

There are no other cases since nn is odd, and 2m=q⋅n+r2m=q⋅n+r, which means modulo two, q+rq+r equals 00.

Let's analyze in which cases this graph will meet the problem's condition. For the condition to be violated, there must be a solution to the equation qa+(q+1)b=mqa+(q+1)b=m, where 0≤a≤n−r,0≤b≤r0≤a≤n−r,0≤b≤r. Additionally, we fix the condition a+b<n2a+b<n2; this must be satisfied for one of the halves of the partition (since the total number of vertices is nn, the smaller half has fewer than n2n2 vertices when nn is odd). We transform the equation:

qa+(q+1)b=mqa+(q+1)b=m

q(a+b)+b=mq(a+b)+b=m

2q(a+b)+2b=2m2q(a+b)+2b=2m

2q(a+b)+2b=qn+r2q(a+b)+2b=qn+r

q(2a+2b−n)=r−2bq(2a+2b−n)=r−2b

q(n−2a−2b)=2b−rq(n−2a−2b)=2b−r

Let's take a closer look at the rewritten equation. The left side is guaranteed to be an integer ≥q≥q, since n>2a+2bn>2a+2b.

We will show that when r<qr<q, the equation has no solutions. When r<qr<q, since b≤rb≤r, we get that 2b−r≤r2b−r≤r, meaning the right side of the equation ≤r≤r, while the left side equals qq, and in the case r<qr<q, such an equation is unsolvable. Thus, if r<qr<q, we have shown that the graph satisfies the problem's condition.

Let's continue the chain of inequalities, each time weakening the inequality.

(q+1)⋅(n−2a−2b)=n−2a−r(q+1)⋅(n−2a−2b)=n−2a−r

q+1≤n−2a−rq+1≤n−2a−r

q+1+2a+r≤nq+1+2a+r≤n

q+r<nq+r<n

It follows that from the assumption that a solution to the equation exists, we derive the fact that q+r<nq+r<n. From this, it follows that when n≤q+rn≤q+r, there is no solution to the equation, and the graph satisfies the problem's condition.

Let's consider the following strategy: while (n−2)(n−3)2≥m(n−2)(n−3)2≥m, we decrease nn by 22 and reduce the problem to (n−2,m)(n−2,m), isolating two vertices of degree 00. In the end, we will obtain a problem where n(n−1)≥2mn(n−1)≥2m and (n−2)(n−3)<2m(n−2)(n−3)<2m, while nn is still odd.

We will show that in this case (when n(n−1)≥2mn(n−1)≥2m and (n−2)(n−3)<2m(n−2)(n−3)<2m) one of the two conditions (n≤q+rn≤q+r or r<qr<q) will always be satisfied, except for the case when m∈{1,2,4,6,12}m∈{1,2,4,6,12}.

n(n−1)≥2m>n2−5n+6n(n−1)≥2m>n2−5n+6

n−1≥⌊2mn⌋>n−5+6n−1n−1≥⌊2mn⌋>n−5+6n−1

n−1≥q>n−6+6nn−1≥q>n−6+6n

Let's analyze the cases.

  1. q=n−1q=n−1

    In this case, the graph must be complete, and for it r=0r=0, thus the condition r<qr<q is satisfied.

  2. q=n−2q=n−2

    If r<n−2r<n−2, then r<qr<q.

    If 2≤r2≤r, then n≤q+rn≤q+r.

    The extreme case where both conditions are not satisfied is n=3n=3, then m=2m=2.

  3. q=n−3q=n−3

    If r<n−3r<n−3, then r<qr<q.

    If 3≤r3≤r, then n≤q+rn≤q+r.

    The extreme cases where both conditions are not satisfied are n=3n=3, m=1m=1 and n=5n=5, m=6m=6.

  4. q=n−4q=n−4

    If r<n−4r<n−4, then r<qr<q.

    If 4≤r4≤r, then n≤q+rn≤q+r.

    The extreme cases where both conditions are not satisfied are n=7n=7, m=12m=12 and n=5n=5, m=4m=4.

  5. q=n−5q=n−5

    The unweakened condition: 2m>n2−5n+62m>n2−5n+6, thus 2m−qn>62m−qn>6, meaning r>6r>6. Therefore, the condition n≤q+rn≤q+r in this case is always satisfied.

Thus, our strategy has been proven to yield an indivisible graph for odd nn, and m∉{1,2,4,6,12}m∉{1,2,4,6,12}!

By bruteforce, it is easy to show that all possible graphs with m∈{1,2,4,6}m∈{1,2,4,6} will be divisible. For example, for m=2m=2, the possible vertex degrees are either {1,1,1,1}{1,1,1,1} or {1,1,2}{1,1,2}.

And for m=12m=12, it is possible to create a graph on 66 vertices (and for all n>6n>6, we can take this graph and n−6n−6 isolated vertices) that satisfies the indivisibility condition, with degrees {3,3,3,5,5,5}{3,3,3,5,5,5}, which is K6K6 without three edges between the first three vertices.

Thus, the solution for odd nn is No for m∈{1,2,4,6}m∈{1,2,4,6}, with the case m=12m=12 handled separately; otherwise, the strategy works: take the densest graph with mm edges and ≤n≤n vertices, supplemented with isolated vertices.

Now let's solve the problem for even nn. Immediately, we can discard a cluster of cases using the solution for odd:

  • If m∈{1,2,4,6}m∈{1,2,4,6}, the answer is still no.
  • If m=12m=12, the same example will work.
  • If m≤(n−1)(n−2)2m≤(n−1)(n−2)2, we can isolate one vertex and reduce the problem to (n−1,m)(n−1,m), and n−1n−1 will be odd, thus we know that with such parameters a solution always exists.

The only remaining case: 2m>(n−1)(n−2)2m>(n−1)(n−2).

Notice that in this case, if the graph has a partition of vertices into two sets with equal sum of degrees, then both of these sets must have size n2n2, otherwise, the smaller set has size ≤n2−1≤n2−1, and thus the maximum sum of degrees of this set: (n−1)(n2−1)=(n−1)n−22(n−1)(n2−1)=(n−1)n−22, while mm is strictly greater than this number.

Knowing this, we note that in this case, the condition of indivisibility for this graph is equivalent to the condition of indivisibility for the reverse graph, but with the added condition that the vertex sets must have equal size.

In the reverse graph, there will be n(n−1)2−m<n−1n(n−1)2−m<n−1 edges. If n(n−1)2−mn(n−1)2−m is not equal to 0,1,2,4,60,1,2,4,6, we can take an indivisible reverse graph on n−1n−1 vertices and n(n−1)2−mn(n−1)2−m edges, using the construction above. Since this graph is indivisible in general form, it is also indivisible under the added condition of equal set sizes.

It remains to deal with the cases when n(n−1)2−m∈{0,1,2,4,6}n(n−1)2−m∈{0,1,2,4,6}. Here we can recall the restriction n≥12n≥12. It turns out that for n≥12n≥12, any graph with 0,1,2,4,60,1,2,4,6 edges is not just always divisible, but always divisible into two sets of equal size. This is true because in a regular partition, the sizes of both sets do not exceed 66 (if we only take non-isolated vertices), thus we can take a regular partition and supplement it with isolated vertices so that both sets have size 66. And for n>12n>12, we can take a pair of isolated vertices in different sets and reduce the problem to n−2n−2. Thus, in this case, there is no solution to the problem.

Second solution (thanks to [user:SomethingNew] for the alternative solution!):

Let's note/guess/suppose that the graph answer cannot have more than 3 different degrees. More precisely, it is sufficient to consider only graphs with no more than 3 different degrees to cover all possible (n,m)(n,m) where a solution exists.

Let's bruteforce all possible sets of degrees and use the Erdős–Gallai theorem to check whether a graph can be formed from these degrees, in O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n). Checking the indivisibility criterion can also be done quite quickly, given that there are no more than 33 different degrees (they can be arranged in O(logn)O(log⁡n) knapsack items), and using a bitset.

Such a solution already works, but is obviously too slow. However, we can cleverly "tweak" the parameters of the enumeration, trying to guess the properties of the construction so that it works in terms of time.

  • Enumerate the actual nn (without isolated vertices) in increasing order, starting from the first such that n(n−1)2≥mn(n−1)2≥m.
  • Enumerate the maximum degree value only from nn to n−5n−5.
  • Honestly enumerate the number of vertices with the maximum degree, as well as the minimum degree, after which we honestly enumerate the average degree. Of course, add as many break as possible.

It is claimed that such bruteforce will run in O(mm−−√logm)O(mmlog⁡m) time, as all considered values of nn will be O(m−−√)O(m)

Solution
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
#define int long long
#define pb push_back
#define all(x) x.begin(), (x).end()
#define rall(x) x.rbegin(), (x).rend()
using namespace std;
 
vector<pair<int, int>> theSolve(int n, int m) {
  if (m == 1 || m == 2 || m == 4 || m == 6) {
    return {};
  }
  if (m == 12) {
    vector<pair<int, int>> e;
    for (int x = 1; x <= 6; x++) {
      for (int y = x + 1; y <= 6; y++) {
        if (max(x, y) >= 4) {
          e.pb({x, y});
        }
      }
    }
    return e;
  }
  if (n == 6 && m == 11) {
    return {{1, 2},
            {1, 3},
            {2, 4},
            {2, 5},
            {2, 6},
            {3, 4},
            {3, 5},
            {3, 6},
            {4, 5},
            {4, 6},
            {5, 6}};
  }
  if (n == 8 && m == 22) {
    vector<pair<int, int>> e;
    for (int v = 1; v <= 7; v++) {
      for (int u = v + 1; u <= 7; u++) {
        e.pb({v, u});
      }
    }
    e.pb({7, 8});
    return e;
  }
  if (n == 10 && m == 39) {
    vector<pair<int, int>> e;
    for (int x = 1; x <= n - 1; x++) {
      for (int y = x + 1; y <= n - 1; y++) {
        e.pb({x, y});
      }
    }
    for (int x = 1; x <= 3; x++) {
      e.pb({n, x});
    }
    return e;
  }
  vector<pair<int, int>> edges;
  if (n % 2 == 1) {
    while (m <= (n - 2) * (n - 3) / 2) {
      n -= 2;
    }
    int q = (2 * m) / n, r = (2 * m) % n;
    if (q % 2 == 0) {
      for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
        for (int p = 0; p < q / 2; p++) {
          int u = (v + 1 + p) % n;
          edges.pb({v + 1, u + 1});
        }
      }
      for (int v = 0; v < r / 2; v++) {
        edges.pb({v + 1, (v + n / 2) % n + 1});
      }
    } else {
      int d = (n - r) / 2;
      for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
        for (int p = 0; p < (q + 1) / 2; p++) {
          if (v % 2 == 0 && p == 0 && d) {
            d--;
            continue;
          }
          int u = (v + 1 + p) % n;
          edges.pb({v + 1, u + 1});
        }
      }
    }
    return edges;
  }
 
  int rest = n * (n - 1) / 2 - m;
  if (rest == 0 || rest == 1 || rest == 2 || rest == 4 || rest == 6) {
    return {};
  }
  if (m <= (n - 1) * (n - 2) / 2) {
    return theSolve(n - 1, m);
  }
  vector<pair<int, int>> inv_e = theSolve(n - 1, n * (n - 1) / 2 - m);
  for (auto &p : inv_e) {
    if (p.first > p.second) {
      swap(p.first, p.second);
    }
  }
  sort(all(inv_e));
  int i = 0;
  vector<pair<int, int>> e;
  for (int v = 1; v <= n; v++) {
    for (int u = v + 1; u <= n; u++) {
      if (i < (int) inv_e.size() && inv_e[i] == (pair<int, int>) {v, u}) {
        i++;
        continue;
      }
      e.pb({v, u});
    }
  }
  return e;
}
 
void solve() {
  int n, m;
  cin >> n >> m;
  vector<pair<int, int>> e = theSolve(n, m);
  if (e.empty()) {
    cout << "No\n";
  } else {
    cout << "Yes\n";
    for (auto &[a, b] : e) {
      cout << a << ' ' << b << '\n';
    }
  }
}
 
signed main() {
  ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
  cin.tie(nullptr);
  cout.tie(nullptr);
  int t;
  cin >> t;
  while (t--) {
    solve();
  }
}

For those who come after.

Tutorial of Codeforces Round 1079 (Div. 1)

  • FelixArg
  • 6 weeks ago

Another solution for div1 D is to just simulate the brackets until you fail. At that moment change this bracket to something else (because you don't know what to change it to, keep all (at most 3) possibilities. At the end the one closest to (0, 0) will give you the minimum changes required.

362523300

Truth be told, I did not manage to prove that this is correct. It is mostly intuition from the simplified problem of only one type of paranthesis (only change when prefix is negative and resolve the end). Would be great if someone could prove/disprove/hack this solution

      The-Winner
      6 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +3

      Assume you have xx open round paranthesis and yy open square paranthesis. Then the minimum number of operations to get a good string is x+y2x+y2. Take the minimum of these values.

          SmpaelFx
          6 weeks ago, show #

          SmpaelFx
          6 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          Consider how to determine if a sequence is valid. Maintain two sums x,yx,y. For (, x←x+1x←x+1; for ), y←y+1y←y+1, and square brackets do the similar operation on yy. A sequence is valid if and only if x,y≥0x,y≥0 at any moment, and finally x=y=0x=y=0.

          For the single bracket type problem, the optimal strategy is to change ) to ( when sum<0sum<0. For two types of brackets, an invalid ) might be changed to one of (, [, or ]. Maintain these three results, and in the subsequent process, avoid extra operations as much as possible unless none of these states remain valid.

          → Reply

Nachia

Nachia

← Rev. 2 →  
+122

We can do Div1E in O((n+m)log(n+m))O((n+m)log⁡(n+m)) time (with no factors of kk).

We want to find the maximum dd where:

  • Given ptpt , psps , and ℓ≥1ℓ≥1 . We can assume spssps+1…sps+ℓ−1=tpttpt+1…tpt+ℓ−1spssps+1…sps+ℓ−1=tpttpt+1…tpt+ℓ−1 .
  • There exists a substring of ss which matches to the pattern : (tpttpt+1…tpt+ℓ−1tpttpt+1…tpt+ℓ−1) + (an arbitrary character) + (the following dd characters)

Consider all the positions qq of substrings of ss such that sq−ℓ…sq−2sq−1=tpttpt+1…tpt+ℓ−1sq−ℓ…sq−2sq−1=tpttpt+1…tpt+ℓ−1 . These positions form a segment in the suffix array of the reversed ss . Associate the suffix of the reversed ss (sq−1sq−2…sq−1sq−2…) with a suffix of ss (sq+1sq+2…sq+1sq+2…). Now we just need to find the maximum LCP between the following characters in tt and sq+1sq+2…sq+1sq+2… , but the value qq must be taken from the corresponding segment of the suffix array of the reversed ss .

Eventually, the task is reduced to a typical query: find the minimum element in a subarray (al,al+1,…,ar)(al,al+1,…,ar) but no less than xx (and also the maximum but no greater than xx). This can be solved using a wavelet matrix.

362520685

I found a binary search solution for div2A. The idea is that suppose an infinite sequence of integers where iith element of the sequence is i−d(i)i−d(i) where d(i)d(i) equals to the sum of the digits of ii. Then this sequence will be non-decreasing. So we can binary search for xx in this sequence taking the lower bound and upper bound in a way such that any xx in the given range 1≤x≤1≤x≤101099 can be found if present. If xx is present, then the answer is 1010, otherwise 00. You can see my submission 362438894 for implementation. In this code, I have also utilized the fact that xx must be divisible by 99 to be in the sequence, which i guess was not necessary to check.

→ Reply

Bramer_x
6 weeks ago, show (+3) #

Bramer_x
6 weeks ago, hide # |
 
0

My idea for div2 D was to speedup the bruteforce method and avoid recalculation. When iterating over k for j=i+k⋅aij=i+k⋅ai, for a given ii, we will do the calculations considering the pair (ai,i mod ai)(ai,i mod ai). This means that for any index jj that we encounter in further iterations in which (aj,j mod aj)=(ai,i mod ai)(aj,j mod aj)=(ai,i mod ai) we can skip it, as we have previously calculated its pairings. The idea behind that is that for all indices with this pairing, they would go through the same jj's and have the same value for contributing to the equation.

When finding such a pair which has not been calculated previously, we can maintain a set with all indices jj where aj=aiaj=ai and incrementing the answear if j−ai⋅ajj−ai⋅aj is in the set.

My code: 362526064

The complexity is roughly O(nn−−√log(n))O(nnlog(n)), i feared receiving a tle but then i noticed that it would be fast enough with 5 minutes for the contest ending and got AC.

→ Reply

    0

    You can use an unordered_set instead of an set, which has an amorized time complexity of O(1) per insertion and query. The only moment when you want to use a normal set is when you want to remove/query the largest/smallest element, or you care about the elements being sorted, neither of which you do in this case.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Bramer_x
      3 weeks ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      Bramer_x
      3 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      After i made this comment i had actually tried submiting with a unordered_set to see if it would gain some performance, but it actually performed worse. I had this experience a lot when switching between set and unordered_set because normally the test cases are made in such a way that all the element may go to the same bucket or it happens that you get really unlucky and some buckets start getting a little too dense, so i normally use set by standard.

      I also noticed that i didn't really needed a set, and could just use an auxiliary vector that said if an element was already considered in some other iteration, thus making the algorithm work in O(nn−−√)O(nn). Here are the subimissions:

      on-contest with set (734ms): 362526064

      using unordered_set (1171ms): 362541967

      optimized with no set (359ms): 362705070

      → Reply

        2 weeks ago, show #

        »
        stasbadzi2
        2 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 2 →  
        +3

        You're right — when accessed many times, but not with many distinct elements unordered_set offen does end up slower thans set, but over 90% of the time you can just use a bitset which is always faster (you just have to remember that bitset<200'000> b is linear and so is setting it to 0). If you change your vector to bitset, you go down to 328ms (366405742). The difference here is small, but visible and in some cases can be much larger, so it's usually better to use a bitset or a vector<char> when you need to dynamically change the size

        → Reply

0 
void solve(){
    //for div2D
    int n;
    cin>>n;
    vector<int> a(n);
    int ans=0;
    forn{cin>>a[i];}
    forn{
        int c=a[i];
        int j=i-c;
        int count=1;
        while(j>=0&&count<a[i]){
            if(a[j]==count){
                ans++;
            }
            j-=c;
            count++;
        }
        j=i+c;
        count=1;
        while(j<n&&count<=a[i]){
            if(a[j]==count){
                ans++;
            }
            j+=c;
            count++;
        }
    }
    print(ans);
}
→ Reply

+16

There's a O(mnΣ−−−√)O(mnΣ) for div1E, where Σ=26Σ=26 is the size of alphabet. For a string with length LL, there's at most LΣLΣ different possible strings, and we maintain all of their positions in SAM. When the length LL exceed a threshold BB, do the bruteforce in O(n)O(n) mention in the solution for E1.

Here the time complexity is mB⋅Σ⋅B2+mB⋅nmB⋅Σ⋅B2+mB⋅n, which is O(mnΣ−−−√)O(mnΣ) when B=nΣ−−√B=nΣ. The first part of the solution is not full and the solution is easy to squeeze.

→ Reply

In the Div2A editorial, why do we need to check the range from x to x + 83? The problem states that x is between 1 and 10^9, so shouldn’t we only need to check i in the range from x to x + 81?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    udayvalera
    6 weeks ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    udayvalera
    6 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    That is something you will notice in various solution. It is just a safe redundancy to make sure there is no runtime or logical error. The most used example that I have seen is when the author creates an array a[N], where N would be 2e5 + 10. Although, the size may never exceed 200,000. It is just there to make sure that any unexpected error does not appear.

    In this case, it does not matter that we check for 82 and 83. It is just there. If you observe other submission for same problem, you may find many using x to x + 100.

    → Reply

Tried hell a lot to debug div2B, pls help

my approach: in a map i store, the indices where each element appeared in the permutation array. an integer pivotIndex, indicates, till now, which was the last element from which we copied values. [to be precise, if I am at some index i, then pivotIndex represents the index of the element used to correct the p[i].] i iterate through the permutaion array, there will be three possibilities

  1. p[i] = a[i], then pivotIndex = i; i++;
  2. p[i] != a[i], then a. mp[a[i]] < i and mp[a[i]] >= pivotIndex, then we can copy required value, new pivotIndex = mp[a[i]]; i++;

b. mp[a[i]] > i, then check if till required index, all elements are a[i] only, if yes, very nice new pivotIndex = mp[a[i]];

else not possible

// my soln code
void solve()
{
    num n; cin >> n;
    vector<num> p(n);
    map<num, num> mp;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        cin >> p[i];
        mp[p[i]] = i;
    }
    int pivotIndex = 0;
    vector<num> a(n); for(auto& x : a) cin >> x;
    bool solved = true;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        if(p[i] == a[i]) {pivotIndex = i; continue;}
        else
        {
            if(i > 0 && mp[a[i]] < i && mp[a[i]] >= pivotIndex) {p[i] = a[i]; pivotIndex = mp[a[i]];}
            else if(i < n-1 && p[i+1] == a[i]) {p[i] = a[i]; pivotIndex = i+1;}
            else if(i < n-1 && mp[a[i]] > i)
            {
                int j = i+1;
                bool possible = true;
                while(j!=mp[a[i]])
                {
                    if(a[j] != a[i]) {possible = false; break;}
                    else {p[j] = a[i]; j++;}
                }
                if(possible) {p[i] = a[i]; i=j-1; pivotIndex = mp[a[i]];}
                else {solved = false; break;}
            }
            else {solved = false; break;}
        }
    }
    cout << (solved ? "YES\n" : "NO\n");
}
→ Reply

I tried C) fraction by say let say we start with 10,14 now its not 2/3 so we find next 2/3 ratio which is 8 and 12, now alice wants to make it 7 ( 8-1) as soon as possible, while Bob only has to made 14 to 12, so Bob needs 2 Alice needs 3, thats not possible even if Alice plays first, so Bob wins, this approach was not working for this big sample test case, 7000000000000000 10487275715782582, as I was not able to find which is the next closest p/q=2/3 ratio values of p and q are, so I used a loop for it but that loop is not stopping so I took some help from claude but still its not working this is my https://codeforces.com/contest/2197/submission/362586660 

code const fs = require('fs');

const data = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8').trim().split(/\s+/);
let w = 0;

const T = Number(data[w++]);

for (let t = 0; t < T; t++) {

    const p = BigInt(data[w++]);
    const q = BigInt(data[w++]);

    const half = p / 2n;

    if (q === half * 3n) {
        console.log('Bob');
        continue;
    }

    if (p >= q) {
        console.log('Alice');
        continue;
    }

   // let count = (half * 3n > q) ? half - q / 3n : 0n;

    let count = (half * 3n > q) ? (half * 3n - q + 2n) / 3n : 0n;

    const req1 = (half - count) * 2n;
    const req2 = (half - count) * 3n;

    const diff1 = (p - req1) + 1n;
    const diff2 = q - req2;

    if (diff2 < 0n) {
        console.log('Alice');
        continue;
    }

    if (diff1 + 1n <= diff2)
        console.log('Alice');
    else
        console.log('Bob');
}

    hey here is how i thought about the problem

    bob will in if any value k is such that by p-k/q-k = 2/3 meaning we took 2/3 this ratio then multiplied both up and down with some value 'm' then added k to both . so by substracting k now from p,q they both reaches the desired value where p/q=2/3

    now lets say p=2m+k and q=3m+k so q-p=3m+k-2m-k=m there fore we now need to check if p and q gives exactly same reminder with m or not( k) if so then we are happy and then we check when p and q divided with m does we really get 2 and 3 ....and we are done this was my approach but it didnt worked too sad

This is my thought process when writing Div1 D:

We can obviously think of a wrong simple greedy approach: remove all legal bracket matches, and it is easy to find that the remaining ones need to be paired up, so we can directly divide the number of remaining ones by 2

We can find an obvious counterexample to prove the approach is incorrect: when the sequence of parentheses we remove is )(, no matter what, we need to perform two operations to make it valid.

However, we found that this situation is very rare, and the approach only goes wrong when the parenthesis sequence grows into a pattern like )))(((. Therefore, after special handling, this problem can be solved

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    von_arrow
    6 weeks ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    von_arrow
    6 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +6

    I did something somewhat similar, but more convoluted. In the first traversal, pair up and "remove" those elements — 0 cost. Then, no pair remains and we need at least 1 operation to make each pair. Hence, the answer is at least half of the amount of remaining chars. It turns out this lower bound can almost always be achieved. And if it can't, there is only one more case: the lower bound + 1 (because a single pair requires 2 operations).

    Call the chars '[' and '(' as S (start). Call the charss ')' and ']' as E (end). It is possible to prove that the extra cost occurs iff the amount of Es is odd and the rightmost E occurs before the leftmost S.

    Hence, the problem is reduced to making the optimal pair ups in the first step: let's try to let the leftmost remaining '(' or '[' (i.e S) as to the left as possible. The opposite for ')', ']' (i.e E). A simple greedy works: traverse from right to left in terms of the starting chars. For the ending chars, mantain a stack.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Furioso_Slient
      6 weeks ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      Furioso_Slient
      6 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +3

      Essentially, they are the same.

      The essence of my approach is to pair brackets in the same direction, while yours essentially involves pairing those in different directions.

      It is easy to prove that the ultimate cost of these two approaches is the same. However, my approach does not require actual pairing and can directly compute the answer in O(1)O(1) (of course, during the competition, I was too lazy to write two pointers to determine positions, so I directly sorted and took the index position. Therefore, my code runs in O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n), but it could easily run in O(n)O(n))

      → Reply

My Div2 A solution:

Let's think about denoting yy in the decimal notation. If y=100a2+10a1+a0(0≤ai≤9)y=100a2+10a1+a0(0≤ai≤9), then d(y)=a2+a1+a0d(y)=a2+a1+a0, so x=y−d(y)=99a2+9a1x=y−d(y)=99a2+9a1.

More generally, if yy is denoted as: y=∑i=0n10iai(0≤ai≤9)y=∑i=0n10iai(0≤ai≤9), then x=∑i=1n(10i−1)aix=∑i=1n(10i−1)ai. Note that a0a0 does not appear in xx, because (100−1)a0=0(100−1)a0=0. Since x≤109x≤109, having n=9n=9 is sufficient.

Therefore, for every a=[a1,a2,…,a9]a=[a1,a2,…,a9] such that x=∑i=19(10i−1)aix=∑i=19(10i−1)ai, you have 1010 different numbers of yy that satisfies y−d(y)=xy−d(y)=x (you have 1010 here because you can choose a0a0 freely).

It turns out the representation of aa is unique for xx that can be denoted in the above way.

Proof. The maximum value that can be represented in mm digits is:

M=∑i=1m(10i−1)⋅9=9(10(10m−1)9−m)=10(10m−1)−9m=10m+1−10−9m.M=∑i=1m(10i−1)⋅9=9(10(10m−1)9−m)=10(10m−1)−9m=10m+1−10−9m.

The minimum unit of the (m+1)(m+1)-th digit is 10m+1−110m+1−1, which is larger than MM. This means that you can greedily determine the values of aiai from the higher digits (a9,a8,…,a1)(a9,a8,…,a1) without negative consequences. So, you can consider this aa as some form of positional notation system, where only some set of numbers can be represented.

So, the final answer is 1010 if you can find a suitable aa with the above greedy method. Otherwise the answer is 00.

    For the case where a[i]=sqrt, let's say that k is some a[j] where j then the pair (j,i) will be counted once while iterating for a[j] (as if a[j]>=sqrt we iterate in both directions).

    Similarly if j>i it will be counted.

    #include <bits/stdc++.h>
    using namespace std;
    #define int long long
    #define srt(a) sort(a.begin(), a.end())
    #define srtr(a) sort(a.rbegin(), a.rend())
    #define cyes cout<<"YES"
    #define cno cout<<"NO"
    #define pii pair<int,int>
    #define deb(a) for(int x:a) cout<<x<<" "
    #define rev(a) reverse(a.begin(), a.end())

    vector<int> qry(int k){
        cout<<"? "<<k<<endl;
        int q; cin>>q;
        vector<int> a;
        if(q==0) return a;
        for(int i=0; i<q; i++) {int x; cin>>x; a.push_back(x);}
        return a;
    }

    void solve(){
        int n;
        cin>>n;
        
        vector<int> path_len(n+1);
        path_len[1]=1;
        vector<pii> adj;
        int k=2;
        while(1){
            vector<int> p=qry(k);
            int q=p.size();
            if(q==0) break;
            if(q>=2) adj.push_back({p[q-2], p[q-1]});
            for(int i=0; i<q-1; i++){
                path_len[p[i]]++;
            }
            if(path_len[p[q-1]]==0) path_len[p[q-1]]++;
            k+=path_len[p[q-1]];
        }
        
        cout<<"! "<<adj.size()<<endl;
        for(auto [u,v] : adj) cout<<u<<" "<<v<<endl;
    }

    int32_t main(){
        ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
        cin.tie(0);
        int tc;
        cin>>tc;
        while(tc--){
        solve();
        //cout<<endl;
    }

        return 0;
    }

Can anyone help me find where i am going wrong and a counter example would be really appreciated where my code takes more than (n+m) queries. My logic may be entirely or partially incorrect. Thanks!

In div2D, isn't it a typo?

"If ai≥Bai≥B and aj<Baj<B, then the pair will be counted when we iterate through candidates for j;" — shouldn't it be "candidates for i"?

I assume i < j and for aj<Baj<B, when j is fixed, only indices i>j would be considered (according to strategy "iterate through candidates only forward"). If i would be fixed instead, we're guaranteed there won't be more than ⌈n−−√⌉⌈n⌉ checks (total for both sides, forward and backward) for j ("it is nBnB") and that mentioned pair would be covered.

can someone please explain div2 D in simpler terms

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    -Nebula
    6 weeks ago, show #
    »
    »
    -Nebula
    6 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +3

    we want a[i] * a[j] = j — i

    let's make an assumption, a[i] & a[j] are both larger than sqrt(j — i), then, a[i] * a[j] would be larger than j — i. Hence our assumption is wrong, and the opposite of our claim is true, i.e. either a[i] <= sqrt(j — i) or a[j] <= sqrt(j — i). (If you know DeMorgan's law it'll be clear how we inverted the statement).

    by the previous statement, we can pair an element <= sqrt(n) with an element <= sqrt(n). And, we can also pair an element <= sqrt(n) with an element >= sqrt(n). These two scenarios are allowed, but we can't pair two elements >= sqrt(n).

    iterate through the elements and call the current element x.

    if x >= sqrt(n): we need an element y with a distance d from the current element such that x * y = d. From the equation we see that d is a multiple of x. Hence, x <= d = x * y <= n — 1 (the farthest pair possible)

    rewriting, x <= x * y <= (n — 1) / x --> 1 <= y <= (n — 1) / x, we said that x >= sqrt(n), that means the worst case for the value of y is sqrt(n) (plugging the smallest value of x = sqrt(n) to make (n — 1) / x as large as possible).

    You can iterate through the possible values of y, setting d = x * y, and checking if the element behind the current element by d equals y, or the element in front of you by d equals y.

    If x <= sqrt(n): we want to pair it with an element <= sqrt(n), and not with an element >= sqrt(n) since in the previous part we did that already.

    since we want to pair it with an element <= sqrt(n), we can just brute force this value (call it y). 1 <= y <= sqrt(n). We want an element that equals y with a distance x * y from the current element, rather than checking the indices of y in the array, we can instead use the info that d = x * y and check the element behind me by d and in front of me by d and seeing if it equals y or not. (you need to handle the collisions btw)

    I've commented an another solution below you maybe it'll make sense. Hope that helped :>

    → Reply
»
-Nebula
6 weeks ago, show #

← Rev. 5 →  
+3

Another solution for B div1:

let d be the the distance j — i (d = j — i), x = a[i], y = a[i].

x * y = d.

let x = min(x, y), y = max(x, y).

we know that x <= sqrt(d) and y >= sqrt(d) ==> y ^ 2 >= d.

we also know that d % y = 0, i.e. d = c * y where c is some constant. Hence, y <= d.

since d is the difference of the indices, it's max value is n — 1, we can write it as d <= n for the sake of simplicity.

y <= d <= min(n, y ^ 2), but d = c * y

y <= c * y <= min(n, y ^ 2), dividing by y we get

1 <= c <= min(n / y, y), that worst case for min(n / y, y) is y = sqrt(n). Hence, 1 <= c <= sqrt(n).

we can iterate through the elements of the array supposing the current element is the max between the elements in the pair (i.e. y in the explanation), and brute forcing the value of c to get every possible value of d as d = c * y.

C++ code
void solve(){

    int n;
    cin >> n;

    vector<int> arr(n);
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
        cin >> arr[i];
    }

    int ans = 0;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
        int y = arr[i];
        if(arr[i] >= n)
            continue;
        
        int lim = min(n / y, y);
        for(int c = 1; c <= lim; c++){
            int d = c * y;
            if(i + d < n && arr[i + d] * y == d)
                ans++;
            if(i - d >= 0 && arr[i - d] * y == d && arr[i - d] != y)
                ans++;
        }
    }

    cout << ans << endl;

}
→ Reply

I'm afraid not even the problem writers know what is going on on C2 — Interactive Graph. Evidence of this is the fact that, on their solution, they compute the longest common prefix of the vectors prevprev and curcur, but that is not needed because the longest common prefix always has length cur.size()−1cur.size()−1. Also, they talk about "connected components" on the editorial, which doesn't help much, because the exercise doesn't have connected components.

My take on their solution is the following:

The sequence of paths in lexicographical order simulates a lexicographical DFS traversal of the DAG. Indeed, when you do a query kk, the interactor returns the call stack at the moment kk.

With this, we sketch the general idea: we will do a DFS over the DAG, but we will try to avoid visiting a vertex twice (this way we get linear complexity).

Let's do queries from k=2k=2, then k+1k+1, k+2k+2, and so on, until we visit a vertex for the second time. During that process, let's also keep at what kk a vertex first appeared, and at what kk it left the stack. To do that, we only need the current DFS stack pp and the previous one prevprev. Notice that the longest common prefix of those stacks is always p.size()−1p.size()−1, because pp was made by removing elements from the back of prevprev and then appending a new element.

Whenever we visit a vertex uu for the second time, we already know how many paths start from it: that amount is timeOut[u]−timeIn[u]timeOut[u]−timeIn[u]. So, instead of querying at k+1k+1 now (which will visit those paths), let's go to k+timeOut[u]−timeIn[u]k+timeOut[u]−timeIn[u] instead. This will skip exactly all the paths that start on uu, and this will effectively make our DFS go to the vertex after uu in DFS order.

This algorithm does exactly the walk a DFS (without vertex repetition) would do, and therefore, the edges of the graph will be the ones made by the 2 last vertices of pp (check code for more details).

Submission

→ Reply

Can someone read my solution for Div2/E1 (Interactive Graph — Simple version) and please explain how the number of queries made by my solution never exceeds 32*(n+m)?

I am unable to mathematically prove my solution's optimality as it tends to get quite rigorous at least for me. I am not even sure if my solution is optimal and whether if it might get hacked by some test case even if it gets accepted for now.

Intuition for my solution: I begin with a linear iteration where I start with the first path (i = 1). You can make an observation that while doing so, if a particular vertex appear at the same position in a series of consecutive paths and then it changes its position or does not appear in the next path at all, that means the entire sub-tree from that vertex has already been encountered and we can store the edges involved in that sub-tree since we iterate through all the paths from that vertex for the first time. Then, we also store the number of paths that originate from that vertex in count array (in my solution) because if that vertex appears for another time, we can directly skip to the path i = i+count[vertex] since we have already encountered the entire sub-tree from that vertex so there is no new edge to be stored.

Please go through my solution to understand what I am talking about.

Submission: 364466471

→ Reply
